entries.text: App is awesome with lots of data, ring is lightweight and comfortable, customer service described as helpful and seamless.
Rings don't last long, battery issues starting around 6 months, some users report the AI feature makes up fake data.
Across 101 mentions, users praise sleep tracking and app quality but complain about durability and accuracy.
Would not recommend, walking sensor is off, cannot detect cycling or driving correctly, wildly off from Google Fit.
Love my Ultrahuman ring air, gives awesome health and fitness insight, customer service is excellent.
One year review warning this is not a good product - long-term durability complaint.

Engadget rated the Ring Pro 8.9/10, praising better-looking titanium hardware, a feature-rich charging case (firmware-update recovery, 45-day case battery), and a data-rich app; main knocks were higher price than rivals and no major leap over the Ring Air.

Tech Advisor's Ring Pro review (Editors' Choice) praised solid tracking, a slick subscription-free app, and up to 15-day battery life, but flagged the $479/£429 price as expensive, the design as chunky, and noted software features are largely unchanged from the Ring Air.

Ultrahuman raised roughly Rs 400 crore (~$48M) in a Series C round to fund US re-entry with the redesigned Ring Pro, geographic expansion, and new wrist-based/home health products; total equity raised now exceeds $60M.
Coverage of Ultrahuman's smart ring returning to the US market after the Ring Air was previously blocked over an Oura patent dispute, with the redesigned Ring Pro clearing the way for re-entry.

Multiple app store reviews complain about a glut of ads pushing upgrades to the paid 'Ultrahuman X' tier priced around $15/month, unreliable background syncing, and inability to adjust blood glucose target zones.

Ultrahuman told Ring Pro Kickstarter backers and store customers that shipments are delayed again, now expected to begin August 10, citing extra quality-control checks during production scale-up. This is the second slip from the original June window, and backers voiced visible frustration on Reddit over the repeated delays.
Tom's Guide reported that the subscription-free Ultrahuman Ring Pro is officially launching in the US, framing it as a direct pricing/business-model challenge to Oura.

A July 2026 review piece described the Ring AIR as Ultrahuman's most consistent seller, crediting its detailed sleep, recovery, and heart-rate tracking plus the lack of a monthly subscription for its staying power against rivals.

Ultrahuman HYROX Bengaluru, India's first two-day HYROX event, drew a record 8,200+ participants and 12,000+ spectators, up sharply from prior India editions, with Olympic medallist P.V. Sindhu among the competitors.

A ThePrint feature on the Ultrahuman-branded HYROX Bengaluru race (8,200+ entrants paying roughly Rs 9,000 each) covered a social-media backlash calling the pricing exploitative and a marker of 'class inequality,' countered by others defending it as a fair cost for a fitness challenge; organizers confirmed the next Delhi edition (July 24-26) expects 10,000-10,500 participants, up from 2,600 in 2025.

Notebookcheck reported the Ultrahuman Ring Pro delay now extends beyond Kickstarter backers to direct store orders, with the online store showing shipping pushed to August 15 (backers to August 10); Ultrahuman attributed the two-month slip from the original June date to added per-unit quality-control inspection during ramp-up.

Ultrahuman disclosed that hackers used credentials stolen via malware on an employee's laptop to gain read-only access to an internal analytics system on March 27, 2026, exposing wellness data for about 0.1% of users; the company said no passwords, payment data, the ring hardware, or production systems were affected.

A MakeUseOf opinion piece used the Ultrahuman breach (~700 users affected per the company's own 0.1% estimate) to argue smart-ring wearers should reconsider trusting wearable makers with health data, prompting the author to say she's retiring her own ring; noted Ultrahuman's post-incident hardening (stricter access controls, endpoint security, anomaly detection).

Ultrahuman launched M2 Live, a new metabolic health platform pairing Abbott's prescription-free Lingo continuous glucose monitor with Ultrahuman's Jade AI insights engine, a Metabolic Score, and Food Score; pricing starts at $99/month for two sensors ($129 two-pack, $1,229/year for 26 sensors), available to US adults not on insulin.

NBC Select's two-month hands-on review of the Ring Air praised its light, comfortable fit and actionable, subscription-free data versus the Oura Ring 4, but knocked its slow ~180-minute charge time (vs. 80-90 min for rivals) and an app that leans on confusing jargon ("phase advance," "circadian rhythm") and feels clunky to navigate.

A two-month hands-on Ring AIR review gave it 4/5 stars, praising sleep-tracking accuracy (it flagged an oncoming illness before symptoms appeared), six-day battery life, and titanium durability on hikes and dives, but dinged a steep app learning curve, layout changes after updates, and an inaccurate 'social jet lag' timezone feature.

Aggregated App Store reviews describe being double-charged during a glitchy lifetime-promo signup (later refunded after a second, less friendly support contact) and being locked out of premium on a second device after having to re-register with a different email.

A May 2026 hands-on review called the $479 Ring Pro's 15-day battery life and 45-day-capacity Pro Charging Case a genuine leap over rivals, also highlighting 250-day on-device storage, a dual-core ML chip, and the new subscription-free Jade AI platform rolling out to all Ultrahuman ring owners.

A Run To The Finish review scored the Ring AIR 85%, praising its lightweight comfort, accurate sleep/HR data versus a GPS watch, and no-subscription model, but flagged a short 4-6 day battery life, ~180-minute charge time, and scratches/discomfort during strength training and sleep.

A reader comment on Believe in the Run's Ring Air review said the app's onboarding skipped basic profile/health questions and instead led straight into upsell pop-ups for paid upgrades, prompting the $349 Costco buyer to return the ring the same day; a separate comment on the same page reported the ring's charge dropping to only ~8 hours within 1-2 weeks of use.

Android Central's review called the Ring Air an excellent sleep-tracking companion that's comfortably light once properly fitted, but knocked the app for needing work, unpolished beta fitness features, and a fairly high price.

A detailed switcher review from a longtime Oura user found the Ring Air's sleep tracking clearly less accurate than Oura's (initially misclassifying time spent awake as asleep, missing awakenings, and logging phantom overnight steps), plus intrusive upgrade ads and confusing notification channels; the reviewer requested a refund. A later update noted Ultrahuman fixed the awake-as-asleep bug.

Tom's Guide covered the newly announced Ultrahuman Ring Pro ($479, shipping March) as a flagship Oura Ring 4 competitor, but noted it would not be available in the US at launch, disappointing the US-based reviewer.

Wareable reported Ultrahuman's Ring Pro cleared US Customs and Border Protection by redesigning to a unibody form that sidesteps Oura's asserted patent, enabling a May 15 US relaunch with early-bird pricing from $349; CEO Mohit Kumar discussed engineering around the patent dispute and future plans like lactate-based biomarkers.

Ultrahuman Healthcare holds a 4.8/5 rating across 5 Thingtesting reviews; most reviewers praise comfort, cycle tracking, and the lack of subscription, but one reviewer said sleep tracking 'is really not great at all,' the ring scratches easily, and Ultrahuman keeps adding more paid add-ons for accessories.

Tech Times covered the Ring Pro's worldwide shipping start ($479, no subscription) alongside the newly launched Oura Ring 5, calculating the Ring Pro's 2-year total cost of ownership as roughly $60 lower than Oura's despite a higher sticker price and 15-day vs 6-9 day battery life, while noting Oura's Ring 5 offers phone-GPS live activity tracking that the Ring Pro lacks.
